title: Length vs. Width: The Main Differences • 7ESL
headline: Length vs. Width: The Main Differences
summary: In the world of measurements, two of the fundamental terms we encounter are length vs. width. These dimensions are critical in various fields from construction to sewing, and understanding the difference between them ensures the accuracy and functionality of the items being crafted or analyzed.
keywords: is the longest dimension of an object from end to end.
, often synonymous with breadth, is the narrower side-to-side measurement.
In real estate, we might describe a parcel of land as having a
of 150 meters and a
of 50 meters.
When drafting the specifications for manufacturing, a legal document might state that a widget has a
of 10 cm and a
of 5 cm.
Our new smartphone has a
of 162 mm and a
of 74 mm.
: Think of “length” with “long” and both start with “L”.
: When placing an object horizontally, the horizontal measurement is typically the length, while the vertical measurement is the width.
: For a rectangle, the sides that are longer are its lengths, and the shorter sides are its widths, with both sets of opposite sides being parallel and equal in length.
: On a standard sheet of paper (like A4), the longer side is considered the length, and the shorter is the width.
We measured the
of the new sofa to ensure it would fit along our living room wall; it was exactly 84 inches long.
The runner sprinted the entire
of the track, which is 400 meters from start to finish.
During the construction project, we frequently checked the
of the wooden planks; they had to be cut to a precise 6 feet.
The
of the dress was perfect, falling just below the knee at 36 inches.
For the science experiment, we recorded the
of the shadow every hour to observe how it changed.
The
of the hallway is only 36 inches, making it a tight fit for large furniture.
When designing the table, we decided that a
of 30 inches would allow enough space for comfortable seating on either side.
The
of the fabric bolt was 48 inches, ideal for the curtains we wanted to make.
To pass the safety inspection, the emergency exits must have a minimum
of 22 inches.
The bridge was constructed with a
allowing two vehicles to pass side by side, approximating 16 feet.

refers to the measurement from one end of an item to the other end. In the context of a room, it’s the distance between the front wall and the back wall. For example, the legal length of a property boundary.

, on the other hand, is the measurement from the base to the top of something. If we consider a building, height measures from the ground to the highest architectural feature. For instance, zoning laws often restrict the maximum height of structures.
signifies the measurement across the narrowest part of an object from one side to the other. A common example includes the width of a standard road lane determined by transportation regulations.

can often be interchanged with width, though it sometimes implies a more general measurement of overall wideness. In legal documents, width specifications might be used for the allowable size of a parcel of land.

title: Pint vs. Quart: Understanding Terms for Measuring Liquids • 7ESL
headline: Pint vs. Quart: Understanding Terms for Measuring Liquids
summary: Understanding the difference between a pint and a quart is essential, especially if you engage in cooking, baking, or any activity that involves measuring liquids. Pints and quarts are both units used to measure volume, commonly found in recipes and on packaging for liquids.
keywords: Used in the imperial and U.S. customary measurement systems.
In the United States, 1 pint is equal to 16 fluid ounces, or about 473 milliliters.
In the UK, 1 pint is equal to 20 imperial fluid ounces, or about 568 milliliters (known as an imperial pint).
Commonly used for measuring liquids, especially beverages like beer and milk.
A metric unit of volume.
1 liter is equal to 1,000 milliliters or 1 cubic decimeter.
Used worldwide for a wide range of applications, including beverages, engine displacement, and container sizes.
The liter is not an SI unit, but it is accepted for use with the International System of Units.
1 U.S. pint ≈ 0.473 liters
1 Imperial pint ≈ 0.568 liters
1 liter ≈ 2.113 U.S. pints
1 liter ≈ 1.76 Imperial pints
The size of a schooner can vary by region.
In Australia, a schooner is a beer glass size that is typically 425 milliliters, which is smaller than an Australian pint (570 milliliters).
In Canada, a schooner refers to a large beer glass, but it is not a standardized measure and can be larger than a Canadian pint (20 imperial fluid ounces or about 568 milliliters).
In the UK, a schooner is 2/3 of a pint or approximately 379 milliliters, though it is not a commonly used measure.
A quart is equal to one-quarter of a gallon.
In the U.S. customary system, 1 quart is equivalent to 32 fluid ounces, or about 946 milliliters.
In the imperial system (used in the UK), 1 quart is approximately 40 imperial fluid ounces, or about 1,136 milliliters.
A gallon is a larger unit of volume measurement.
In the U.S. customary system, 1 gallon is equivalent to 128 fluid ounces, or about 3.785 liters.
In the imperial system, 1 gallon is approximately 160 imperial fluid ounces, or about 4.546 liters.
1 U.S. gallon = 4 U.S. quarts
1 imperial gallon = 4 imperial quarts
